title: Methods
text: How do savory food intake and cholesterol jointly predict the presence of key beneficial gut microbial taxa?

title: Methods
text: How do savory food intake and cholesterol jointly predict the presence of key beneficial gut microbial taxa?

title: Methods
text:
2 Data source
The project uses data from Gut microbiome signatures of vegan, vegetarian and omnivore diets and associated health outcomes across 21,561 individuals, representing a subset of the ZOE PREDICT 1 (P1) cohort in the United Kingdom.

Working cohort size: N = 92 participants
Inputs: dietary pattern metrics, clinical biomarkers, and microbiome taxonomic data
Data format: three source .xlsx files merged into a unified analysis matrix

title: Methods
text:
3 Analytical pipeline

Cohort and data assembly
Construct a working analysis dataset from ZOE PREDICT 1 subset data plus reference public metagenomic information.
Data integration and cleaning
Merge all files into feature_matrix.csv to simplify analysis in Python and R.
Taxa binarization
Convert taxonomy strings into per-taxon presence/absence indicators.
Distribution checks
Generate Q-Q plots in R to assess normality and identify skewed variables.
Exploratory associations
Build correlation heatmaps (corrplot) and run PCA to identify dominant features spanning diet, biomarkers, and taxa.
Statistical modeling
Fit logistic regression models for target taxa using savory intake, cholesterol, and their interaction while adjusting for age, sex, and BMI.
Multiple testing correction
Apply Benjamini-Hochberg FDR control across model families.

title: Methods
text:
4 References and tooling

ZOE PREDICT 1 Study
Vegan/Vegetarian/Omnivore Study
R corrplot package
Benjamini-Hochberg FDR paper

title: Results
text:
Savory intake and cholesterol each show significant associations with microbial presence.
Their interaction term is significant and negative, indicating conditional effects.
Higher cholesterol appears to attenuate the positive association between savory intake and focal taxa.
PCA highlights diet and biomarker features as dominant axes alongside selected microbial variables.

title: Results
text:
2 Figure walkthrough

2.1 Fig. 1 Analytical pipeline

Cohort + public metagenomic integration
Data harmonization into feature_matrix.csv
Presence/absence encoding of taxa
Distribution diagnostics (Q-Q plots)
Correlation and PCA screening
Logistic regression with interaction terms and FDR correction



2.2 Fig. 2 Differential taxa abundance: low vs. high savory diet
Taxonomic abundance contrasts suggest substantial cohort-level differences by savory intake category, with stronger enrichment/depletion patterns concentrated in selected taxa.


2.3 Fig. 3 Shannon diversity trajectory over 12 weeks
Longitudinal diversity trends indicate increasing divergence between cohorts over time, consistent with sustained dietary modulation rather than transient shifts.


2.4 Fig. 4 Beta diversity (PCoA, Bray-Curtis)
Ordination-level separation between groups supports community-level compositional differences associated with dietary profile.

title: Results
text:
3 Discussion
Our results support a central interpretation: savory dietary patterns do not act independently of host metabolic context. Instead, cholesterol appears to modulate microbiome response, producing a non-linear and context-dependent relationship between diet and microbial composition.
This interaction framework extends the standard “single exposure to single outcome” approach and provides a mechanistic basis for why microbiome responses to diet can vary across individuals with different host phenotypes.

title: Results
text:
4 Limitations
The current analysis is constrained by:

modest cohort size
self-reported dietary measurements
compositional microbial data rather than absolute abundance
limited strain-level resolution relative to shotgun metagenomics

title: Results
text:
5 Future work
Planned next steps include:

replication in larger and more diverse cohorts
coupling with metabolomics and higher-resolution sequencing
intervention-oriented study designs to test causality
validation of focal taxa as potential diet-response biomarkers

title: Results
text:
6 Notes
This page currently prioritizes content transfer and narrative structure from the source site. In the next step, we can decide which interactive elements to re-introduce.

title: Microbiome x Cardiometabolic Health
text:
Bioinformatics Capstone
n = 92 samples. Host phenotyping includes age, sex, BMI, and clinical biomarkers. ZOE PREDICT 1 (UK subset cohort).

title: Microbiome x Cardiometabolic Health
text:
1 Abstract
Humans have co-evolved with a complex internal ecosystem of microbiota, functioning less as isolated organisms and more as symbiotic vessels for trillions of microbial cells. While diets high in savory, energy-dense foods and elevated cholesterol are well-established risk factors for cardiometabolic disease, the precise role of the gut microbiome as an intermediary remains incompletely understood, particularly in the context of how host metabolic state modifies microbial responses to diet.
We hypothesize that the interaction between savory food intake and serum cholesterol levels predicts the depletion of beneficial gut microbes, providing a mechanistic link to diet-induced dysbiosis. Specifically, we tested whether these effects can be observed in key butyrate- and fiber-processing taxa, including Anaerostipes hadrus, Ruminococcus bicurculans, and Alistipes putredinis.
A cohort (N = 92) was combined with public metagenomic datasets. We implemented an integrated analytical pipeline incorporating principal component analysis (PCA), logistic regression models of microbial presence, and adjustment for age, sex, and BMI, with multiple testing addressed using Benjamini-Hochberg false discovery rate correction.
Logistic regression revealed evidence of associations for savory intake (p = 0.0495) and cholesterol (p = 0.0183), as well as a statistically significant interaction between these variables (p = 0.0266). The negative interaction coefficient suggested that increasing cholesterol can attenuate and ultimately reverse the relationship between savory intake and microbial presence.
These findings support a context-dependent mediator model in which host metabolic state shapes how dietary factors influence microbial composition, and they identify specific taxa as candidates for follow-up validation and microbiome-informed dietary intervention studies.

title: Microbiome x Cardiometabolic Health
text:
2 Introduction
Diet is one of the most powerful environmental determinants of human health, with strong evidence linking dietary patterns and metabolic biomarkers to cardiometabolic disease risk. In particular, diets high in savory, energy-dense foods, alongside elevated serum cholesterol levels, have consistently been associated with increased incidence of metabolic dysfunction, inflammation, and long-term disease outcomes. However, the biological mechanisms that connect these risk factors to disease progression remain incompletely understood.
Recently, the gut microbiome has emerged as a key intermediary between diet and host physiology. Rather than functioning as a passive community, gut microbiota actively metabolize diet-derived substrates and produce compounds that influence energy utilization, immune signaling, and whole-host metabolic balance.
One especially important pathway is short-chain fatty acid production, particularly butyrate. Depletion of butyrate-producing and fiber-degrading bacteria, including A. hadrus, R. bicurculans, and A. putredinis, has been associated with gut dysbiosis and adverse downstream outcomes.
Despite this biological importance, most prior work has examined diet-microbiome and cholesterol-microbiome relationships separately. Our study addresses this gap by testing whether host metabolic context alters the microbiome’s dietary response.
